title: Methods and effects of government intervention in markets
paperName: Cambridge International AS Level Economics (P1 & P2)
summary: 9708 AS — taxes, subsidies, regulation, and price controls.
learningObjectives: Analyse effects of indirect taxes and subsidies.

simpleExplanation.title: Tools to fix markets
simpleExplanation.summary: Governments shift supply or demand curves, set legal limits, or regulate behaviour to correct market failure.
steps: Max price: shortage if below equilibrium.
Min price: surplus if above equilibrium.

flashcards.front: How does an indirect tax affect a supply curve?
flashcards.back: Shifts supply left/up by the tax amount — raises price to consumers, lowers price received by producers.

flashcards.front: How does a subsidy affect a supply curve?
flashcards.back: Shifts supply right/down — lowers consumer price, raises price received by producers.

flashcards.front: Effect of a maximum price below equilibrium?
flashcards.back: Creates excess demand (shortage) — Qd > Qs at the controlled price.

flashcards.front: Effect of a minimum price above equilibrium?
flashcards.back: Creates excess supply (surplus) — Qs > Qd; government may buy the surplus.

flashcards.front: Specific tax vs ad valorem tax?
flashcards.back: Specific: fixed amount per unit (e.g. 50p/litre). Ad valorem: percentage of price (e.g. 20% VAT).
Intervention that makes outcomes worse — e.g. poor information, bureaucracy, unintended consequences.

sections.content: **Intervention methods** (3.2) follows **3.1 reasons**. Show **before/after equilibrium** and **surplus changes**.

sections.question:
The market for petrol is in equilibrium at P = £1.40/litre, Q = 500 million litres/month. The government imposes a specific tax of £0.20 per litre. After the tax, consumer price rises to £1.52 and quantity falls to 460 million litres.

(a) What price do producers receive per litre after tax?
(b) How is the £0.20 tax burden shared?
sections.solution:
(a) **Producer price** = consumer price − tax = £1.52 − £0.20 = **£1.32 per litre**.

(b) **Tax burden sharing**
Consumers pay **£0.12 more** (£1.52 − £1.40).
Producers receive **£0.08 less** (£1.40 − £1.32).

Check: £0.12 + £0.08 = £0.20 ✓

**Incidence:** Consumers bear **60%** of the tax; producers bear **40%** — the side with more inelastic demand/supply pays more (link to 2.2 PED and 2.3 PES).

**Welfare:** Draw supply shifting left by £0.20. Shade reduced consumer surplus, reduced producer surplus, tax revenue rectangle, and deadweight loss triangle (→ 2.5).

steps.caption: Indirect tax shifts supply left — raises price.
Subsidy shifts supply right — lowers price.
